items.title: Spain beat France 2-0 — and the model called the closest coin flip it ever produced
items.imgCaption: Pre-match: the tightest big-match call of the tournament — decided by 0.2 points
items.body:
Spain are in the World Cup final. Mikel Oyarzabal from the penalty spot on 22 minutes, Pedro Porro on 58, and a 2-0 win over France in Dallas that stretches Spain’s unbeaten run to 37 competitive matches — level with Italy’s all-time record.

For the model, this was the strangest kind of test. Pre-match it produced the closest call of the entire tournament: **Spain 35.6%, France 35.4%, draw 29.0%**. A 0.2-point lean — the statistical equivalent of a shrug — and it landed on the right side. The pick is graded, and the tournament record moves to **70 of 101 winners called**.

Be honest about what that means: nobody “predicts” a coin flip. If this match were played a hundred times, the model expects France to win around 35 of them. What a record like 70/101 measures isn’t clairvoyance — it’s calibration: when we say 60%, it should happen about 60% of the time, and the [public ledger](/record/) is how you check we’re not grading ourselves on a curve.

Spain now wait for tonight’s second semifinal — England against Argentina in Atlanta, where the model leans **England 52.6–47.4**, a meaningfully clearer edge than the French coin flip. Whoever emerges, Sunday’s final gives Spain a shot at a second star, and gives Italy’s 37-match record one more game in the crosshairs.
items.factcheck: result witnessed + API events cross-checked; unbeaten-run and record figures from the adversarially fact-checked Jul 13-14 match articles; pick numbers from the published pre-match page

items.title: Premier League 2026-27 Preview: Can Arsenal Repeat at 77.7%?
items.imgCaption: Premier League 2026-27 — season preview from 5,000 simulations
items.body:
The 2025-26 Premier League ended with Arsenal’s first title in 22 years, and then the league lost its mind. Guardiola walked away after a decade and six titles. Liverpool sacked the man who won them the 2024-25 championship. Chelsea hired the coach Real Madrid discarded after 233 days. Tottenham went from final-day survival to breaking their transfer record twice in five days. Ten of twenty clubs will start 2026-27 with a manager they didn’t have last August. Our model simulated the new season 5,000 times. The numbers below are what it believes — including the places we think it’s blind.

## Arsenal at 77.7%: the number that defines our season

Arsenal won it with 85 points, 26 wins and a +44 goal difference, the title sealed on May 19 when Man City could only draw 1-1 at Bournemouth — Haaland’s stoppage-time equalizer confirming, rather than delaying, the inevitable. The model makes the champions 77.7% to repeat, 99.0% for the top four, 83 expected points, Elo 2063.8. That Elo sits almost 93 points clear of second-placed City. It’s a monstrous edge.

Is it too high? The honest case against: Arsenal have barely spent. Hincapié’s loan was made permanent for ~£34.5m, Meslier arrived free from Leeds, and the summer’s exits — Kiwior to Porto, Hein to Bremen — were pre-agreed conversions, not fresh business. Meanwhile City dropped a British-record £116m on Elliot Anderson. Elo can’t see transfer ledgers; it only sees results.

But results are exactly why 77.7% reads fair rather than inflated. The chasing pack didn’t merely fail to close the gap — it detonated itself. City lost Guardiola, Bernardo Silva (free, to Real Madrid), Akanji and Aké in one summer. Liverpool are onto their third manager in three seasons and lost Salah, Robertson and Konaté in one window. Arteta’s machine kept its spine and its manager. Standing still is a strategy when everyone around you is on fire.

## The real race: three clubs, one coin

City at 82.1% for the top four are close to a lock even in transition — 12.9% for the title is the model saying post-Pep City remain elite but are no longer a repeat machine, which feels right given the outflow, even with Maresca arriving on a three-year deal (Chelsea pocketing reported £17m-plus in compensation) and Anderson’s £116m arrival. Haaland — 27 goals last season, contract to 2034 — flirted with Spain in the spring but has publicly recommitted. Treat the noise as noise.

Below them is the tightest race on the board: Aston Villa 50.6%, Man United 46.9%, Liverpool 45.3% for the top four. Three clubs, one coin flip, and the model’s reasoning differs sharply on each.

United are its biggest six-month Elo climber at +91, built on Carrick’s 11 wins in 16 after January and a third-place finish that earned him a deal through 2028. Here’s our objection: that +91 is pure momentum. As of mid-July United have sold Højlund to Napoli for ~£43m, let Casemiro go, loaned Onana out again — and signed nobody. A squad priced on a hot half-season with a thinner striker corps is exactly the profile Elo overrates. We think 46.9% is a couple of points too generous.

Liverpool cut the other way. Elo 1910.8 remembers fifth place and the chaos of Slot’s May 30 dismissal; it can’t yet see that Iraola — who just dragged Bournemouth to sixth and the club’s first European qualification in its 127-year history — is a genuine coaching upgrade, or that Isak, the £125m British-record signing, gets a manager built for him. Jacquet (£55m from Rennes) and Victor Muñoz (~£35m) patch the departures. If any of the three beats its number, it’s Liverpool.

Villa at 50.6% are the boring, correct favorite: fourth last season, Watkins’ 16 goals, and the scarcest asset in England right now — continuity. Bournemouth at 20.6% carry the residue of a departed manager; the +73 Elo surge was Iraola’s work, and Kirkland Rose inherits the squad without him or Senesi.

## Tottenham: the team the model can’t price

The full arc bears repeating. Frank sacked on February 11 with Spurs 16th. Tudor’s 44-day interim: seven games, no league win. De Zerbi in on April 1 — five years, third head coach of the season. Survival secured on the final day, 1-0 over Everton, 17th on 41 points, sending West Ham down instead.

Then the summer: Mateus Fernandes for a club-record £85m from relegated West Ham, beating United to the deal after Real Madrid pulled out. The record lasted days — Tonali arrived at £92.5m up front, up to £100m, on a six-year deal. Add van Hecke at £52m, Robertson, Senesi and Dubravka on frees, Bissouma released after 111 appearances.

This is the model’s largest blind spot, and we’ll say so plainly: its rating still carries the scars of a 17th-place season and cannot see roughly £230m of new spine until it plays. Either the steepest Elo climb of 2026-27 starts in August, or this is the most expensive panic buy in Premier League history. Load Spurs into the [what-if lab](/) and watch how far the table bends when you correct for it.

## The bottom: brutal to the new boys, suspicious of Sunderland

Hull, back after nine years thanks to McBurnie’s 95th-minute Wembley winner, get no romance: 99.6% relegated, Elo 1532.9. Ipswich, straight back up on 84 points, sit at 82.9% — and the model can’t know whether O’Neil, appointed June 23 after McKenna’s departure, softens or worsens that. Coventry, champions with 95 points under Lampard in their first top flight since 2000-01, are 72.5%.

Coventry is where we’d argue. A 95-point runaway champion with a settled, award-winning manager is a different animal from a play-off winner or a promoted side that just changed coach; their +54 Elo climb is still in progress. If one of the three stays up, it’s them. Sunderland at 19.9% looks strange for a team that finished seventh and reached the Europa League — until you notice Elo 1735.9 is still bottom-six material and Thursday football is a tax on thin squads.

## Movers and wildcards

The [movers](/movers/) board tells the summer’s story in one line: United +91, Bournemouth +73, Sunderland +56, Forest +55, Coventry +54 — and Chelsea -52. Forest made the sharpest hire of the window: Glasner, fresh from winning the Conference League with Palace and only the third coach ever to win both it and the Europa League, appointed July 6. Palace replaced him with Sage from Lens. Chelsea’s -52 is the ugliest number on the site — Maresca walked out on New Year’s Day with the club fifth, and Alonso arrives off his 233-day Madrid flameout, with Cucurella sold to Real for up to £51.8m and Palestra (up to £47m) his first signing. Two decorated coaches, one damaged rating.

And two stories stay open: Salah remains a free agent after Egypt’s World Cup exit, Saudi clubs circling, nothing confirmed; relegated West Ham reportedly need to raise ~£100m beyond the Fernandes sale, with the Bowen links to Chelsea, Liverpool and United still rumor — he’s contracted to 2030 and hinting he stays.

## What we’ll be graded on

Three claims, in public: Arsenal at 77.7%, Hull at 99.6%, and the Villa–United–Liverpool coin flips at 50.6/46.9/45.3. We’ve told you where we’d overrule the machine — Liverpool up, United down, Coventry alive, Spurs unpriceable. The full table, week by week, lives at the [full Premier League forecast](/league/premier-league/), and every call gets scored on the [track record](/record/). The season starts in August. The grading already has.
items.factcheck: 30 verified claims (5 rejected by the adversarial gate); model numbers from snapshot 2026-07-13

items.title: La Liga 2026-27 Predictions: Can Mourinho Stop Barcelona?
items.imgCaption: La Liga 2026-27 — season preview from 5,000 simulations
items.body:
Barcelona won the 2025-26 title in the cruelest way available: a 2-0 Clásico on 10 May, Rashford in the 9th minute, Ferran Torres in the 18th, with three matches to spare. Ninety-four points to Real Madrid’s 86, a second straight title, a 29th overall. Madrid’s answer was to hire José Mourinho. Our model has already played the 2026-27 season 5,000 times, and it doesn’t think that’s enough.

## Barcelona at 61.7% — belief in the machine, not the squad sheet

The headline from the [full La Liga forecast](/league/la-liga/): Barcelona 61.7% to win a third straight title, 99.9% top four, 86 expected points, Elo 1951.6. That Elo is what back-to-back championships buy you, and it’s doing all the work here — because the squad the model is implicitly rating no longer exists up front.

Lewandowski is gone at 37, walking on a free after Barcelona offered renewal on roughly halved wages. The replacement plan is speed, not presence: Anthony Gordon arrived in late May for €80m including add-ons (€70m up front) on a five-year deal, and Karim Adeyemi’s move from Dortmund — €22m initial, add-ons to €29m — was done by 10 July, with a Camp Nou presentation set for 16 July. Both are wingers. The No. 9 plan is Julián Álvarez, and as of mid-July that plan is not signed (more below).

So here’s our first honest caveat: 61.7% assumes Barcelona’s goals transfer seamlessly from a departed striker to two fast arrivals and a centre-forward they haven’t bought yet. Elo is a results engine; it prices what happened, not who left. If the Álvarez deal collapses and Ferran Torres (16 league goals last season) is the starting nine in a title defence, 61.7% will look generous by October.

## Mourinho, 13 years later, and where the model is blind on Madrid

Madrid’s season was chaos in a blazer. Xabi Alonso, hired in summer 2025 after Ancelotti left for Brazil, was gone by mutual consent on 12 January. Álvaro Arbeloa finished the season, then departed on 9 June. Two days later the board met under Florentino Pérez and paid Benfica’s ~€15m release clause for Mourinho: three seasons to June 2029, in the building on 13 July, the first day of preseason, 13 years after his 2010-2013 stint ended.

And he has rebuilt the squad his way, fast. Cucurella from Chelsea for an initial €55m plus ~€5m in add-ons. Bernardo Silva, 31, free after nine years at Manchester City. Konaté, 27, free after his Liverpool contract ran out. Dumfries for €20m — Madrid triggered his release clause on 5 July, before it started climbing in five-day steps toward €30m, so they paid the floor. Average age of the four: 28.75. ESPN calls it a deliberate “win now” build, and that’s exactly what it is — every signing solves a named problem for next May, none is a bet on 2030.

The model gives Madrid 34.2% title, 99.4% top four, 82 xPts, Elo 1922.9. Our position: that 34.2% is light. Madrid’s Elo carries the full wreckage of a three-coach season, and it cannot see four ready-made internationals or a manager whose entire brief is raising the floor. If the window closed today, we’d argue Madrid’s true number is closer to 40 — which is precisely the kind of call the [track record](/record/) exists to grade.

The overhang is Vinícius. Contract to June 2027, reportedly wanting around €30m a year against a ~€20m offer, talks resuming after the World Cup — and free to negotiate with foreign clubs from 1 January. Both sides reportedly still want a renewal, but a star forward’s unresolved future in January is exactly the kind of thing that turns a Mourinho season combustible.

## Fourth place is the real race

Atlético are the model’s awkward middle: 3.5% title, 82.6% top four, 71 xPts. Near-lock for Champions League, non-factor for the title — and the Álvarez standoff is the reason you can’t price them tighter. Simeone stays (contract to 2027, matching Koke’s one-year renewal), and he says the Álvarez situation will “sort itself out” this summer. It has to, one way or the other, by 1 September.

Below them is the tightest line in the forecast: Villarreal 38.3% for the top four, Betis 29.7%. Villarreal finished third on 72 points, then swapped benches — Marcelino out on 1 June, Íñigo Pérez in from Rayo on the same day, signed to 2029. Pérez is the most interesting managerial bet of the summer: Rayo’s +67 Elo climb over six months is substantially his work, and now he inherits a Champions League squad. We’d take the 38.3% side of that coin flip, and not reluctantly. Rayo, meanwhile, handed the vacancy to ex-Eibar coach San José on 18 June, and the model quietly worries about regression there.

## The bottom: nostalgia has a price

The romance story of 2026-27 is the promoted trio — Racing Santander back after 14 years, Deportivo La Coruña back after eight (promotion sealed with a 2-0 over Valladolid on the penultimate matchday), Málaga back after eight via a 2-1 playoff final against Almería. The model’s response to all that history: Depor 69.7% to go straight back down (Elo 1546.3), Málaga 54.2% (1568.6), Racing 47.9% (1581.9).

Málaga is the case worth arguing about. They’re the biggest six-month Elo mover in the league at +85 — see the [movers](/movers/) board — and the model still makes them likelier relegated than not. That’s the eternal promoted-club tension: momentum says up, baseline says down, and the baseline usually wins. Last season’s baseline certainly did — Oviedo went first on 11 May, Girona and Mallorca followed on 23 May, and Mallorca went down despite Muriqi scoring 23 league goals, second only to Mbappé’s 25.

The incumbent in trouble is Elche at 21.5% (Elo 1623.4). Eder Sarabia resigned at the end of May after keeping them up in 15th; Martín Anselmi arrived on 13 June on a one-year deal with an objectives-linked extension. A new coach on a short contract at the weakest Elo of any non-promoted side in our relegation table is a fragile combination.

## The Álvarez swing state

One transfer moves this whole forecast more than any other. Álvarez has told Atlético he wants out before the window closes. Barcelona have personal terms agreed on a reported five-year deal, saw a ~€100m bid rejected, and are preparing €130-135m with variables toward €150m — against Atlético’s demand of €150m up front. A Real Madrid bid, reportedly viewed as a spoiler, was also rebuffed; Atlético have reportedly gone as far as a FIFA complaint, while Laporta has publicly confirmed the offer and called it time-limited, with a €210m TV-rights advance available to fund it. If he signs, Barcelona’s 61.7% is probably conservative. If he doesn’t, the gap to Madrid’s 34.2% compresses sharply. Run both branches yourself in the [what-if lab](/).

## What we’ll be graded on

Four falsifiable calls. Barcelona favourites but no lock at 61.7%. Madrid’s 34.2% too low — we say Mourinho’s floor-raising beats Elo’s memory of the Arbeloa months. Villarreal over Betis for fourth, 38.3 to 29.7. Depor most likely down at 69.7%.

The checkpoints: the season opens 16 August, Barcelona at home to an Athletic Club now coached by Edin Terzić — the 2024 Champions League finalist with Dortmund, signed to June 2028 as Valverde’s successor — though Barça note the opener could slip a week if their players reach the World Cup semifinals or final. First Clásico at Camp Nou on 25 October, matchday 10. The return at the Bernabéu on 9 May, matchday 35 — three weeks before the maths gets settled. Last season the Clásico decided the title in May. This one is set up to do it again, except now the man on the away bench has spent his whole career winning exactly these.
items.factcheck: 24 verified claims (4 rejected by the adversarial gate); model numbers from snapshot 2026-07-13

items.title: Serie A 2026-27 Predictions: Can Anyone Stop Inter?
items.imgCaption: Serie A 2026-27 — season preview from 5,000 simulations
items.body:
Serie A kicks off on 23 August, and Italian football arrives at it in a strange mood. In March the national team lost a World Cup playoff in Bosnia on penalties after a 1-1 draw — a third consecutive World Cup missed, the first former champion ever to sit out three in a row. Gennaro Gattuso left the Italy job three days later. Gabriele Gravina resigned in April, and Giovanni Malagò was elected FIGC president on 22 June with 68.58% of the vote. The league that opens under that cloud has responded the only way Serie A knows how: by firing, swapping and reshuffling almost every bench in sight.

Massimiliano Allegri was dismissed by Milan the day after they fumbled fourth place on the final day, then resurfaced at Napoli on a deal to 2029 — Aurelio De Laurentiis tweeted "Benvenuto Max!" — replacing Antonio Conte, who left by mutual consent a year before his contract expired. Ruben Amorim, sacked by Manchester United in January after a 14-month, 63-game spell, took the Milan job on 16 June. Maurizio Sarri went to Atalanta, Gattuso to Lazio, Domenico Tedesco to Bologna, and Fiorentina, Sassuolo, Torino and Monza all changed hands too. Only a few benches — Chivu's Inter, Gasperini's Roma, Fàbregas's Como, Spalletti's Juventus — carry over. Into that churn we ran 5,000 season simulations. The [full Serie A forecast](/league/serie-a/) is live; here's the argument.

## The title: Inter, and the question is only how much

Inter won the 2025-26 scudetto — their 21st — with 87 points, sealing it on 3 May with a 2-0 home win over Parma and three matches to spare. Lautaro Martínez took the Capocannoniere with 17 goals in 30 appearances, his second, with Marcus Thuram adding 13 more. The gap to second-place Napoli was 11 points. The model responds accordingly: **69.1% title probability, 96.6% top four, 80 expected points, 1888.6 Elo** — nearly a hundred points of rating clear of anyone else.

Here's where we'll take a position against our own number: 69.1% describes the team that won 87 points, not the one that starts on 23 August. Denzel Dumfries is gone to Real Madrid, who triggered a €20m release clause on 5 July — a clause with a quirky escalator that rose every five days toward €30m, which Madrid simply beat to the punch. Sommer, Acerbi, de Vrij and Darmian all left as free agents when their contracts expired on 30 June. Josep Martínez is promoted to No.1 with Ivan Provedel signed for €3m as experienced cover — and Provedel is coming off shoulder surgery that may keep him out until August. Chelsea even hijacked Inter's move for Serie A's Defender of the Season. That's a spine being stripped, and Inter's Elo has already shed 39 points over six months. We'd argue the true title number sits closer to 60 than 70. The counter-argument, and the reason the model isn't panicking: nobody behind them has done anything to earn the difference.

## The race that matters: four Champions League tickets, six claimants

This is the tightest market on the board: **Roma 62.4%, Napoli 52.6%, Juventus 45.8%, Atalanta 40.7%, Como 38.5%, Milan 32.1%** for top four. Thirty points separate first claim from sixth. Someone with a genuine case misses out.

Roma at 62.4% is the model's quiet conviction pick. Gasperini finished third with 73 points in his debut season, Devid Malen scored 14 goals, and year two of a Gasperini project has historically been where the pressing patterns compound. Napoli at 52.6% is the number we'd push back on hardest. Yes, their Elo fell 42 points as they finished 11 behind Inter — but they were defending champions, they won the Supercoppa in December (2-0 over Bologna, a David Neres double), and they've replaced Conte with Allegri, the most reliable floor-raiser in Italian football. Elo prices the late-season fade; it cannot price a coaching upgrade that happened in June. We'd take Napoli above Roma on football grounds even though our own simulation disagrees.

Juventus at 45.8% is a referendum on continuity as strategy. Spalletti finished sixth with 69 points and missed the Champions League entirely — and Juventus responded by extending him to 2028, reportedly worth upward of €6m a season, in April. Kenan Yildiz is staying: Arsenal's June enquiry was firmly rejected, the valuation reported around €100m, and Fabrizio Romano called the exit door shut. But Dusan Vlahović walked out as a free agent after talks collapsed over a reported €8m-a-season demand, and as of mid-July he's still unsigned, with Barcelona reportedly his preference amid Bayern, Chelsea and Newcastle links. A team that couldn't score enough to make the top four just lost its only recognised No.9 for nothing.

Atalanta at 40.7% is the number we trust least. They sacked Palladino on 9 June after roughly six months despite a seventh-place finish, and Sarri arrives on a three-year deal off a mutual-consent Lazio exit that followed clashes with Lotito. That's not continuity — it's a full stylistic rebuild dressed as a coaching hire, and the Elo (down 52 in six months) is closer to the truth than the simulation's residual respect.

And then Como. Fourth place, 71 points, one ahead of Milan, secured with a final-day 4-1 win over Cremonese while Milan lost 2-1 at home to Cagliari. A first-ever Champions League qualification, in only their second season back in Serie A after a 21-year exile. Fàbregas himself cited "15 players who are all under 23"; Tasos Douvikas scored 14 goals. Como are the only riser on our [movers](/movers/) board, +36 Elo against a sea of red. The 38.5% is not a sentimentality discount — it's a Champions League-workload tax on the league's youngest squad. It's also the number we'd most enjoy being wrong about.

## Milan: the biggest faller, the biggest variance

Milan's -67 Elo is the largest six-month decline in the league, and it's earned: fifth place, no Champions League, a manager sacked. But the number is entirely backward-looking. Amorim arrives on three years to 2029 with an option for 2030, and his first signing smashed the club record — Gonçalo Ramos for €74m plus add-ons, potentially €80m+, obliterating the €49.5m paid for Rafael Leão in 2019. Leão himself is the unresolved question: recent reporting says Amorim has sanctioned a departure, Milan want at least €60m, Tottenham have talked to his agent, and the player will decide after Portugal's World Cup. A Milan with Ramos, a settled system and no midweek Champions League football could blow through 32.1%. A Milan where the Amorim-at-United version shows up could undershoot it. That 32.1% is the single number on this page most likely to look silly by May — in either direction. Run your own version in the [what-if lab](/).

## The bottom: Monza drew the short straw

Monza came back up the hard way — a 2-0 home playoff win over Catanzaro, a 2-0 defeat in the return, promotion on superior regular-season finish at 2-2 aggregate — an immediate bounce-back from relegation, now handed to Ivan Jurić. The model is brutal: **62.0% relegation probability, 1544 Elo**, the weakest team in the division. Behind them, **Frosinone 47.1%, Lecce 46.8%, Cagliari 28.6%**. Last season's floor was 18 points (Pisa); Jurić's job is to make Monza's fight look nothing like that.

## What we'll be graded on

Four falsifiable calls: Inter retain the title (69.1%, though we think the honest number is lower); Roma make the top four ahead of Napoli (the one we'd overrule ourselves on); Atalanta miss the Champions League despite the 40.7%; Monza go straight back down. Every one gets scored in May, next to everything else on our [track record](/record/). The model is a statistical engine, not an oracle — it can't see Allegri's whiteboard or Amorim's redemption arc. That's exactly why we write down where we disagree with it before a ball is kicked.
items.factcheck: 25 verified claims (6 rejected by the adversarial gate); model numbers from snapshot 2026-07-13

items.title: Bundesliga 2026-27 Season Preview: Predictions From 5,000 Sims
items.body:
## Bayern at 94%, and why that isn’t complacency

Bayern didn’t win the 2025-26 Bundesliga so much as lap it. The title was sealed on 19 April with a 4-2 win over Stuttgart, four matches to spare, and the final margin read 89 points to Dortmund’s 73. The number people will still be quoting in a decade is 122 league goals — smashing their own record of 101 from 1971-72, edging past Real Madrid’s 121 in 2011-12, and leaving only Torino’s 125 from a 40-game 1947-48 season above them in top-five-league history. Harry Kane took a third straight Torjägerkanone with 36 goals. Deniz Undav was second on 19. Nobody else cleared 17.

Our model runs 5,000 simulations of 2026-27 and hands Bayern the title 94.0% of the time, with top four at 100.0% and 82 expected points. Their Elo sits at 2000.9 — 166 points above Dortmund, the next-best side. If anything, 94% is the model being polite. Bayern kept Vincent Kompany (contract to 2029), kept the squad that scored 122, and then added Ismael Saibari from PSV for a reported EUR 50-55m on a deal to 2031. Champions who win by 16 points usually coast through the window. This one spent.

The only loose thread in Munich is the one that matters most. Kane’s EUR 65m release clause lapsed on 31 January — Uli Hoeness confirmed it publicly — but his contract runs only to summer 2027, and extension talks are still expected. If something drags Bayern under 94%, it won’t be a rival. It’ll be a contract page.

## The real race is 45.9% against 45.0%

Second through fifth is where this league gets interesting, and the tightest call on our whole board is Stuttgart against RB Leipzig for the last Champions League spot: 45.9% top-four for Stuttgart, 45.0% for Leipzig, Elos of 1763.8 and 1761.9, both projected to 57 points. A statistical coin flip. We’ll call the toss anyway: Stuttgart.

Here’s the case. Leipzig’s rating was earned by a team that no longer quite exists. They finished third on 65 points, qualified for the Champions League — and sacked Werner anyway on 17 June, announcing Demichelis five days later on a contract to June 2028. Loïs Openda’s Juventus loan became a permanent ~EUR 40.6m sale once Juve’s top-10 finish triggered the obligation. The reinvestment is sensible — Gladbach captain Rocco Reitz for EUR 21m, Brajan Gruda back on another Brighton loan — but this is a rebuilt attack under a brand-new coach, and Elo can’t see any of it. It just remembers 65 points. Stuttgart, fourth on 62, kept the league’s second-best scorer in Undav (19 goals, 7 assists) and kept their continuity. When the math says coin flip, continuity is our tiebreaker.

Above both, Dortmund at 84.3% top-four (3.5% title, 65 xPts) looks comfortable but is quietly the most date-sensitive number we publish. Niko Kovac stays after the runner-up finish, yet the summer traffic has been outbound: Karim Adeyemi to Barcelona for EUR 22m plus EUR 7-9m in add-ons (Dortmund kept a 35% sell-on), Julian Brandt gone on a free after his deal expired 30 June, Yan Couto loaned to Como with a purchase option. The arrivals are young — Gadou, 19, from Salzburg for EUR 19.5m plus bonuses, left-back Kaua Prates from Cruzeiro — and the whole projection leans on Serhou Guirassy still wearing yellow. His release clause, reported at just under EUR 40m and valid only for a select group of clubs, carries a 15 July expiry; Fenerbahçe, Juventus and Aston Villa have all circled, and Lars Ricken’s “We have no offer for Serhou. We also don’t intend to let him go” is exactly what sporting directors say in both universes. If Guirassy walks, 84.3% goes stale the same day.

## Leverkusen third? That’s the number we trust least

On paper the model still ranks Leverkusen best of the rest: 1.6% title, 70.5% top four, 62 expected points, Elo 1804 — clear of Stuttgart and Leipzig. We think that’s a rating haunted by a team that’s leaving the building. Leverkusen finished sixth and missed the Champions League. Kasper Hjulmand lasted nine months — appointed 8 September, out 4 June, 24 wins from 48. The new man is Carles Martínez Novell, ex-Toulouse, on a two-year deal from 1 July, and per Kicker and Sky he got the job after Andoni Iraola and Filipe Luís both said no. Álex Grimaldo has gone to Atlético Madrid for around EUR 20m fixed (add-ons taking it to EUR 23-25m — Leverkusen accepted under their EUR 30m floor because he wouldn’t extend beyond 2027).

The reinvestment is real: EUR 32m on Portugal U21 winger Afonso Moreira from Lyon, signed to 2031. But a club on its third head coach inside twelve months, selling core players under contract pressure, doesn’t feel like a 70.5% top-four side. The market already agrees: Leverkusen’s Elo has shed 33 points in six months, the third-biggest drop in the league. Our suspicion is it has further to fall — and that Elo lag, not Bayern’s 94%, is where this preview is most likely to look smart or silly by Christmas.

## The most romantic bottom half in years — and the model is unmoved

The newcomers are a storyteller’s dream. Schalke are back after three years as 2. Bundesliga champions on 70 points, sealed with two games to spare, and they’re keeping Miron Muslic — hired from Plymouth Argyle in 2025, since extended to 2028. Elversberg, from a Saarland town of roughly 13,000 people, beat Preussen Münster 3-0 on the final day (one from Bambase Conte, two from David Mokwa) to steal second on goal difference from Paderborn and become the 59th club in Bundesliga history. Paderborn then won the play-off against Wolfsburg 2-1 on aggregate to return for the first time since 2019-20 — the same tie that relegated Wolfsburg for the first time ever, ending 29 consecutive top-flight seasons since 1997.

The model’s response to all that romance: Schalke go down in 66.7% of simulations, Elversberg in 64.6%, Paderborn in 62.3%. Look at the Elos, though — 1519.7, 1523.4, 1525.4. Six points apart. The ordering is noise; the message isn’t. On average, two of the three go straight back down. The genuinely interesting number is Köln at 35.5%. Wagner took over as interim for the final seven games after Lukas Kwasniok’s sacking, went one win, three draws, three defeats, kept Köln up in 14th, and was rewarded with a permanent deal to 2028. Seven games of survival football is a thin CV, and a one-in-three relegation probability says the model has noticed.

## Movers, and what we’ll be graded on

The six-month [Elo movers](/movers/) list is a preview in miniature: Frankfurt -60, Werder -50, Leverkusen -33, Union Berlin -30, Dortmund -29, with Augsburg (+30) the lone climber. Frankfurt’s slide is exactly why the Adi Hütter reunion matters — he returns 1 July on a contract to 2029, back at the club he took to a Europa League semi-final in 2018-19, last seen being dismissed by Monaco in October 2025. Union, 30 points down, hand the rebuild to Mauro Lustrinelli, the Swiss title winner from Thun, also from 1 July.

The season starts 28 August — pushed back about a week by the World Cup — and runs to 22 May 2027, with the German window closing 31 August, so the opening matchday kicks off with the market still open. Our calls, on the record: Bayern at 94.0%; Stuttgart edging Leipzig by nine-tenths of a point; Leverkusen’s 70.5% flagged as too generous; two of three promoted clubs down; Köln closer to the trapdoor than their run-in suggested. Every number comes from the same 5,000 simulations you can inspect in the [full Bundesliga forecast](/league/bundesliga/), stress-test in the [what-if lab](/), and hold against our [track record](/record/) next May.
items.factcheck: 25 verified claims (6 rejected by the adversarial gate); model numbers from snapshot 2026-07-13

items.title: Ligue 1 2026-27 Predictions: PSG's Grip and the Race Behind
items.body:
Ligue 1 stopped being a title race on 13 May, when PSG beat Lens 2-0 to clinch a fifth consecutive championship — a record-extending 14th — with a match to spare. Seventeen days later they retained the Champions League in Budapest, riding out a 1-1 draw with Arsenal after Havertz opened and Dembélé equalized from the spot, then winning the shootout 4-3 when Gabriel missed the decisive kick. Back-to-back European champions, 76 domestic points, a six-point cushion over Lens. That's the baseline everyone else starts from.

## The title question is a margin question

Our model's 5,000 simulations put PSG's title chance at 97.2%, top-4 at 99.9%, with 81 expected points — five more than they needed last season. Their Elo sits at 1967.9. Lens, the next-best team in France, are at 1767. A 200-point Elo gap is not a lead; it's a different weight class.

Which matters, because PSG's summer is genuinely turbulent. Gonçalo Ramos has gone to Milan for €74m fixed — a Milan club record, potentially past €80m with bonuses. Bradley Barcola's camp spent the summer exploring an exit against a ~€150m valuation, with Liverpool and Arsenal circling, though the most recent reporting says PSG have told Liverpool he is not available. Lee Kang-in to Atlético is reportedly agreed at just over €40m including bonuses, but not official. Coming the other way: Lucas Digne, whose sub-€10m release clause PSG are triggering for a backup role behind Nuno Mendes, and — maybe — Monaco's Maghnes Akliouche, where two bids have been rejected and the clubs sit €15m apart, €35m offered against a €50m ask. Everything is paused until France's World Cup ends.

So is 97.2% too confident about a champion rebuilding its front line mid-reign? Honestly, no. Elo can't see squad churn — that's a real blind spot — but the cushion is so vast that PSG could lose Barcola *and* Lee and still start every week as heavy favorites. The number that ages badly this season won't be PSG's.

## The race that actually matters

Behind the champions, the model sees a seven-team scrap for three Champions League places: Lens 63.6% top-4, Lille 48.4%, Lyon 40.7%, Marseille 32.1%, Rennes 32.1%, Monaco 31.8%, Strasbourg 28.0%. That cluster between 28 and 41 is the tightest in any league we model.

Lens carry the strongest number and the weakest case. The résumé is real — 70 points, runners-up, a first-ever Coupe de France under Pierre Sage. But Sage is gone, replaced by Dino Toppmöller, the ex-Frankfurt coach, on a deal to 2028, and five players walked out for nothing: Saint-Maximin, Thomasson, Said and Sarr as end-of-contract frees, Masuaku back to Sunderland after his loan. Elo remembers the 70 points; it cannot see the exodus or the new voice in the dugout. 63.6% is too high. I'd price Lens closer to Lille's number.

Lille (48.4%) are the league's most fascinating variable either way. Genesio took them to third and Champions League qualification, then left; the replacement is Davide Ancelotti, on a two-year contract, in his first European head-coach job after Botafogo in 2025. A rookie coach inheriting a European campaign is precisely what an Elo system cannot price — the upside and the downside are both live.

Lyon (40.7%) keep Fonseca and lose Endrick, whose loan had no buy option — "Real Madrid, without a doubt, will want him back," as Fonseca put it — after 8 goals and 8 assists in 21 games. Making Kamara permanent at €4.1m plus bonuses is tidy business, but replacing Endrick's production is the whole question.

Marseille (32.1%) are the one I'd fade hardest. They're the league's biggest Elo faller at -48 over six months, they missed the Champions League, and Genesio — hired 1 July — is their second head-coach appointment since February, after Beye arrived mid-season and lasted barely five months. The model is already down on them; I'd go lower.

Monaco (31.8%) are the opposite call. A 7th-place finish cost Pocognoli his job after eight months, and the model is punishing that season at face value. But Filipe Luis arrives off a 2025 Copa Libertadores title with Flamengo, Ansu Fati has been made permanent for ~€11m after 12 goals in 30 appearances, Balogun scored 13 last season, and CEO Thiago Scuro says the club is ready to simply keep Akliouche unless PSG meet the €50m ask. If Akliouche stays, this is a top-4 squad wearing a mid-table number.

Rennes (32.1%) had the summer of a well-run club: Habib Beye's mid-February sacking gave way to Franck Haise and a Europa League finish, Esteban Lepaul — the league's top scorer, 21 goals split between Angers and Rennes — gets a full season, Thomasson arrived free from Lens, and the £55m-plus-£5m sale of their 20-year-old France U21 defender to Liverpool (the second-most expensive defender in Liverpool's history, behind only Van Dijk) was agreed back in January.

## The bottom: the model already knows the newcomers

The relegation board reads: Le Mans 79.8%, Troyes 65.9%, Angers 48.6%, Le Havre 30.2%. The two promoted clubs own the two lowest Elos in the league — Le Mans at 1492.4, Troyes at 1521.1 — and the model treats their survival as the exception, not the base case. That's brutal on Le Mans, back in the top flight for the first time in 16 years after losing only four Ligue 2 matches, and on Troyes, champions with 67 points. It's also probably right: they replace Nantes, down after 13 straight top-flight seasons, and Metz, down after one — and the step up from Ligue 2 is the most reliably punishing jump our data measures.

Angers at 48.6% is the quiet grim line — they watched their own top scorer finish the season at Rennes, and the model rates their squad barely above the promoted pair.

Then there's Nice, who survived 16th place only through a 4-1 aggregate playoff win over Saint-Étienne — and whose summer got worse. The sale to American investors collapsed at the final hour after the 15 June deadline passed. INEOS stays for now, the club remains for sale, no player is untransferable — Moffi and Diop especially — and roughly €40m in sales must land before anyone new arrives. The model can't price a fire sale that hasn't happened yet. Watch their line on the [full Ligue 1 forecast](/league/ligue-1/) as the window closes; it will move.

## The mover paradox

The six-month Elo [movers](/movers/) tell one story cleanly: Paris FC +91 is the biggest climb in the league, ahead of Le Mans +61, Auxerre +50 and Lorient +39, while Marseille (-48) and Brest (-42) fall fastest. Note the Le Mans paradox — the league's second-fastest riser is also its likeliest relegation. Both are true: Elo measures trajectory, relegation odds measure level, and a brilliant Ligue 2 season still leaves you 275 Elo points below Lens. If you want to see how many points a hot start would buy them, run it in the [what-if lab](/).

## What we'll be graded on

Season runs 23 August to 29 May. Our falsifiable calls, on the record: PSG at 97.2% — anything but a sixth straight title is a headline miss. Lens top-4 at 63.6%, which we've said in print looks a size too big. Both promoted clubs down more often than not. Marseille underperforming even a modest 32.1%. Monaco outperforming 31.8%. Come May, check every one of them against our [track record](/record/) — the model doesn't get to forget what it said in August, and neither do we.

items.title: France–Spain tonight is the closest call a model can produce
items.sourceTitle: France vs Spain Prediction: World Cup 2026 Semifinal Preview
items.imgCaption: One Elo point apart — the model's tightest big-match call of the tournament
items.body:
The World Cup semifinal kicks off tonight in Dallas (19:00 UTC), and our model has spent a month separating teams only to find two it cannot: **France at Elo 2009, Spain at 2010**. One point. The reach-the-final probabilities land at **50.1% against 49.9%** — championship odds 27.0% and 26.8%. We've called 69 of 100 winners this tournament and named all four semifinalists before the quarter-finals; this is the first match where the honest answer is a shrug.

The shape of the game is less ambiguous than the winner. Spain arrive on a 36-match unbeaten run with one goal conceded all tournament; France have won all six, conceding twice, with Kylian Mbappé on eight goals and a 20th career World Cup goal already banked. Possession against transition, in its purest available form.

What a coin flip actually promises: whoever advances, the model's number was defensible — and whoever loses, half the internet will call the model wrong. That's the deal with 50/50s, and it's why every pre-kickoff number is frozen on the [record](/record/) where nobody, including us, can quietly adjust it afterwards.

The winner gets England or Argentina, who settle the other semifinal tomorrow. Full pre-match analysis on [cup26matches.com](https://cup26matches.com/en/blog/france-vs-spain-prediction-semi-final/), our World Cup 2026 site.
items.factcheck: all figures from the adversarially fact-checked Jul 13 match article (75 verified claims) + model probabilities.json

items.title: Villa hijack the Manzambi deal — and the timing is sharper than the fee
items.sourceTitle: Aston Villa hijack Newcastle's pursuit of £49m Johan Manzambi from Freiburg
items.imgCaption: Johan Manzambi — 20, Freiburg's No 44, fresh from Switzerland's World Cup quarter-final run
items.body:
Aston Villa have jumped ahead of Newcastle for Freiburg's Johan Manzambi, matching the **£49m** deal Newcastle had verbally agreed, per the Guardian and Sky Sports. The 20-year-old Switzerland midfielder — 7 goals and 9 assists in 47 Freiburg appearances, and a starter in the World Cup run Argentina ended on Saturday — reportedly waited out Switzerland's tournament exit, then chose Villa and their Champions League football. The deal is close, not signed.

The model's read: the timing is the story. Villa enter the season at **Elo 1921, third in the Premier League by rating**, with a **50.6% top-4 probability** and 65.5 expected points across 5,000 simulated seasons. Right at the coin flip — and that's precisely where an upgrade buys the most. The top-4 curve is steepest around 50%, so the same +20 Elo is worth roughly twice the probability to Villa that it would be to Arsenal (99.0%) or a mid-table side. With Amadou Onana injured, the midfield is also exactly where Villa's sims leak points.

Elo itself won't budge on signing day — ratings pay for results, not press conferences, which is why summer windows always show up late on the [movers page](/movers/). But you can preview it in the [what-if lab](/): slide Villa up 20 points and watch who pays. Mostly Man United (46.9%) and Liverpool (45.3%), bunched within 11 Elo points of Villa.

For Newcastle — **eighth by rating (Elo 1837.5), 9.3% top-4**, and per Sky the tenth player to turn them down in two years — the miss cuts twice: the club sitting 84 rating points above them, on the right side of the cut line they're chasing, just got stronger with the player they scouted first.
items.factcheck: transfer details verified vs Sky Sports + Guardian syndication 2026-07-14 (fee, verbal Newcastle agreement, WC wait, Onana injury, 10th-rejection stat); model numbers from snapshot 2026-07-13

items.title: Alonso's first Chelsea decision: hold the line at 8%
items.sourceTitle: Xabi Alonso wants Enzo Fernández to stay at Chelsea
items.imgCaption: Enzo Fernández, 24 — wanted by Real Madrid, priced at £120m, currently at the World Cup with Argentina
items.body:
Xabi Alonso used his first press conference as Chelsea's new head coach to say he wants Enzo Fernández to stay — with **Real Madrid**, his own former club, circling and Chelsea reportedly setting a **£120m** price. Fernández himself is otherwise occupied: he plays a World Cup semifinal against England with Argentina tomorrow.

The model's table explains Alonso's urgency better than any press-room quote. Chelsea open 2026-27 at **Elo 1831 — tenth in the Premier League by rating** — with an **8.0% top-4 probability** and 53.9 expected points. Read that again: the model has Chelsea behind Bournemouth, Brighton, Newcastle and Brentford. That's not an insult, it's arithmetic — Elo only knows results, and Chelsea's results have been mid-table for two years. The 8% isn't zero (one sim in twelve gets there), but it prices a club that needs most things to go right.

Which is the real argument for holding the line on Fernández, 24 and the squad's best midfielder. The asymmetry: a club at 8% loses more probability from losing its spine than a club at 90% gains from adding to one. £120m rebuys transfers; it doesn't rebuy the two windows of ratings climb that walk out with him. Rebuilds compound or they collapse — there's little in between at this end of the table.

And every semifinal minute Fernández plays in Dallas tomorrow is Real Madrid's problem now too: nothing moves a price tag like a World Cup. The model will grade Alonso's stance the only way it knows how — results, one matchweek at a time, on the [public record](/record/).
items.factcheck: Alonso first-presser stance, Real Madrid interest, £120m price verified vs ESPN + Yahoo/Standard 2026-07-14; model numbers from snapshot 2026-07-13; Fernández age/number from API-Football
